behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: The failure is at a module handoff boundary (manifest validation -> loader/import path), not just pure schema logic. A unit test of the validator would miss regressions where the loader still imports the plugin despite validation failure (MOCK-MISS/MOCK-HIDE). No real network/subprocess/OS primitive is required beyond local in-process wiring, and no AI output is involved, so System/Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp plugin directory with one invalid plugin (missing required manifest field) whose entry module has a visible side effect (e.g., writes a marker file or toggles shared state on import). Run the real loader over that directory and assert: (1) loader reports manifest rejection, (2) side-effect never occurs, (3) onLoad is never called.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on interaction between dependency resolution and lifecycle invocation order, so single-function unit coverage is insufficient for the stated failure boundary. It does not require real OS/network/subprocess behavior, and correctness does not depend on AI/ML output. Higher levels were considered and rejected because the behavior text is strictly about deterministic local ordering of hook calls.
behaviors.test_description: Set up two real plugin fixtures in a temp directory: A depends on B. Each onLoad appends its name to a shared in-memory log/file. Run loader and assert load succeeds with order [B, A].

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection alone could be unit-tested, but the behavior explicitly requires loader-level outcome ('reports the cycle and loads neither'), which is cross-module orchestration (graph analysis + load suppression). No real subprocess/network/OS primitive is necessary; no AI dependency. System/Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by the behavior text’s deterministic local graph semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Create plugin A and B manifests with mutual dependencies and entry modules that emit import side effects. Execute loader and assert: (1) cycle error/report includes A<->B, (2) neither plugin import side effect occurs, (3) neither onLoad runs.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is ordering across loader stages (import/execute -> onLoad), which requires real module wiring, not only unit logic. Although it touches runtime import semantics, it can be reproduced in-process without subprocess or external infrastructure, so System is not required. No AI/ML output is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Build a plugin whose entry module sets a global/shared flag during module execution, and whose onLoad asserts that flag is already set (or records observed state). Run loader and assert onLoad is invoked only after import side effects are visible.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its onLoad hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: This is loader orchestration across dependency graph + error isolation boundaries. Unit tests for try/catch paths are too narrow and can miss real continuation/skip behavior across multiple plugins (MOCK-MISS). No real network/subprocess/OS behavior is required; no AI dependency; no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Create plugins A, B, C where B depends on A and C is independent. Make A's onLoad throw. Run loader and assert: (1) A failure is reported, (2) B is not initialized due to dependency, (3) C still imports and runs onLoad successfully.
